previous_training_programs_table.institution_and_country: Massachusetts General Hospital, Boston, MA, USA
previous_training_programs_table.purpose: Six-week clinical observership in the Department of Internal Medicine focusing on inpatient ward rounds, multidisciplinary case discussions, and exposure to US-style electronic medical records and quality improvement practices. I shadowed attending physicians and residents on the general medicine service, participated in morning reports and morbidity & mortality conferences, and completed a small case-based presentation on the management of new-onset atrial fibrillation in hospitalized patients.

previous_training_programs_table.program_clinical_research: Short-term research internship in global health
previous_training_programs_table.institution_and_country: London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine, London, United Kingdom
previous_training_programs_table.purpose: Six-week research internship with the Centre for Global Chronic Conditions. I worked under the supervision of a senior epidemiologist on a secondary data analysis project examining hypertension control rates in urban versus rural primary care clinics in South India. My responsibilities included data cleaning in Stata, basic descriptive and multivariable regression analyses, and drafting a preliminary results section for a manuscript in progress.

goals_statement.goal_1:
During the International Scholars Program, my first major goal is to gain structured, hands-on exposure to how large academic medical centers integrate clinical care, teaching, and research within internal medicine. I completed my MBBS at Christian Medical College in Vellore in 2022 and have since been working as a junior resident in general medicine at a tertiary-care hospital in Bengaluru. While I have had solid clinical exposure, I have not yet seen, from the inside, how a well-resourced academic center systematically organizes team-based care, resident education, and quality improvement initiatives.

Specifically, I want to observe and understand: (1) how multidisciplinary rounds are run on general medicine and subspecialty services; (2) how responsibilities are divided between interns, residents, fellows, nurse practitioners, and attending physicians; (3) how electronic health records, clinical decision support tools, and standardized order sets are used to reduce variation in care; and (4) how feedback is provided to trainees in real time. I hope to take detailed notes, collect example protocols and checklists (where allowed), and have at least a few one-on-one conversations with faculty or chief residents about how they think about balancing efficiency with teaching.

My plan is to maintain a daily reflective journal during the program in which I document at least one concrete workflow or teaching practice that is different from what I currently see in India. At the end of the program, I would like to synthesize these observations into a short presentation that I can share with my colleagues and supervisors back home, highlighting 3–5 specific practices that could be realistically adapted to our setting, such as structured sign-out, standardized admission order sets for common conditions like heart failure, or brief bedside teaching models that do not significantly prolong ward rounds.
goals_statement.goal_2:
My second key goal is to strengthen my practical research skills, particularly in the areas of study design, data management, and critical appraisal of the literature, so that I can more confidently lead small clinical research or quality improvement projects in my home institution.

Although I have participated in one short-term global health research internship in the UK, my role was mostly limited to data cleaning and running analyses as instructed. I still feel unsure about how to formulate a research question that is both clinically meaningful and methodologically feasible in our context, how to design data collection tools that are robust but realistic, and how to navigate issues such as confounding, bias, and missing data when working with real-world clinical datasets.

During the program, I hope to:
1. Attend any available research seminars, journal clubs, or works-in-progress meetings, paying particular attention to how faculty and trainees frame their research questions and justify their methodological choices.
2. Work closely with a mentor or supervising faculty member on a small, well-bounded project (for example, a retrospective chart review or a secondary analysis of an existing dataset related to hypertension, diabetes, or heart failure, which are highly relevant to my patient population in India).
3. Improve my ability to use at least one statistical software package (R or Stata) more independently, including writing basic scripts, generating reproducible tables and figures, and documenting my analysis steps clearly.

My concrete outcome target is to leave the program with either (a) a near-complete abstract suitable for submission to a regional or international conference in internal medicine or global health, or (b) a clearly defined and IRB-feasible research proposal that I can implement when I return to my home institution, including a draft data collection form, a proposed analysis plan, and a realistic timeline.
goals_statement.goal_3:
My third major goal is to deepen my understanding of health systems and to develop practical ideas for improving continuity of care and patient education for people with chronic non-communicable diseases (NCDs) in low- and middle-income settings like India.

In my current work at a busy government-affiliated hospital in Bengaluru, I frequently see patients with poorly controlled hypertension, diabetes, and heart failure who are admitted with preventable complications—hypertensive emergencies, diabetic foot infections, or decompensated heart failure. Many of them report fragmented care, inconsistent follow-up, and limited understanding of their medications. I believe there are lessons to be learned from how academic centers in high-income countries organize outpatient follow-up, patient education, and multidisciplinary team support.

While in the International Scholars Program, I want to:
1. Observe outpatient clinics that manage chronic NCDs, paying attention to how providers assess adherence, address social determinants of health, and involve nurses, pharmacists, and social workers.
2. Collect examples of patient education materials (brochures, handouts, or digital resources) that are written in clear, accessible language, noting how risk is communicated and how self-management is encouraged.
3. Learn how care coordinators or case managers help high-risk patients navigate follow-up appointments, referrals, and medication refills.

I would like to leave the program with at least a rough prototype of a patient education tool or structured counseling checklist for newly diagnosed hypertensive or diabetic patients that could be adapted into Kannada and used in our outpatient department. I also hope to identify at least two specific process changes (for example, a standardized discharge summary template that explicitly lists follow-up dates and warning signs, or a simple registry of high-risk patients) that I can propose to my department when I return.
goals_statement.goal_4:
Beyond clinical and research skills, an additional goal for me is to build a small but meaningful international professional network that I can maintain over the long term. Coming from a middle-class family in Kochi and being the first physician in my immediate family, I have had relatively limited exposure to mentors outside India. I would like to change that in a purposeful and respectful way.

During the program, I will make an effort to introduce myself to residents, fellows, and faculty members whose interests overlap with mine (particularly in internal medicine, cardiometabolic disease, and health systems). My aim is not to request favors but to learn about their career paths, the challenges they faced in training, and how they think about balancing clinical work with research and teaching. I want to be honest about my own background and long-term goal of contributing to strengthening chronic disease care in public-sector hospitals in India.

Concretely, I hope to:
1. Have at least three scheduled one-on-one conversations (over coffee or lunch) with mentors or senior trainees to discuss training pathways, fellowship options, and collaborative research opportunities.
2. Exchange contact information with peers and mentors and ask permission to stay in touch via email or professional platforms such as LinkedIn.
3. Identify at least one potential collaborator who might be interested in partnering on a small cross-site project in the future, such as adapting a patient education tool or piloting a simple quality improvement intervention in my home institution.

My long-term vision is that these relationships will not only support my own professional development but also create a bridge between our institutions, making it easier for future trainees from my hospital to participate in similar exchange or observership programs.
goals_statement.goal_5:
Another goal is to significantly improve my confidence and fluency in professional medical English communication, both spoken and written. While my formal education has been in English and I read international journals regularly, I still sometimes struggle with presenting complex cases concisely, asking questions in large group settings, and writing clearly structured emails or abstracts that meet the expectations of international audiences.

During the program, I plan to:
1. Volunteer to present at least one brief case or topic during a team meeting, journal club, or informal teaching session, even if it feels intimidating. I want to practice organizing my thoughts, highlighting the most relevant clinical points, and responding to questions on the spot.
2. Ask a mentor or senior resident to observe one of my presentations and give me specific, constructive feedback on my clarity, pacing, and organization.
3. Draft at least one extended piece of professional writing in English—such as a conference abstract, a short reflection for a departmental newsletter, or a draft introduction for a research manuscript—and request feedback from my supervisor.

I will keep a small notebook (or digital document) where I jot down useful phrases, feedback, and examples of well-structured clinical reasoning that I observe in rounds or conferences. By the end of the program, I would like to feel more comfortable speaking up during discussions, introducing myself confidently, and writing emails and abstracts that are clear, polite, and appropriately formal.
goals_statement.goal_6:
Finally, I want to use this program as an opportunity for personal growth—specifically, to become more adaptable, resilient, and culturally sensitive when working in unfamiliar environments. I have lived most of my life in Kerala and Karnataka, primarily within familiar cultural and healthcare systems. Although I have traveled abroad briefly for conferences and a short internship, I still sometimes feel self-conscious about my accent, my background, and whether I "fit in" in Western academic settings.

During the program, I plan to push myself gently outside my comfort zone by:
1. Actively participating in team discussions rather than staying quiet, even when I am unsure if my contribution is perfect.
2. Seeking to understand the perspectives of patients and colleagues from diverse cultural, racial, and socioeconomic backgrounds, and reflecting on how my own assumptions and biases may differ.
3. Managing the practical challenges of living abroad (navigating public transportation, handling administrative tasks, and managing time zone differences with family at home) in an organized and proactive way.

I will write weekly reflections about what surprised me, what felt challenging, and what I learned about myself. My hope is that by the end of the program, I will not only have gained professional skills but also a stronger sense of self-efficacy and confidence that I can adapt to different healthcare systems and still hold on to my core values of empathy, humility, and a strong commitment to serving patients in resource-limited settings.
